channel.title: Comments for page "Dr. Michaels"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Dr. Michaels"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I likensome parts of this article a lot, but there are things I really didn't understand, and one of my pet peeves is authors who leave unexplained mysteries that they purposefully don't explain in the comments. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is possibly my favourite DoMC skip. And that's saying a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon reread, I'm unsure how I missed that the first time around. But although there's a lot going on under the surface, it's not a lot of worthwhile reading. The article's appeal hinges entirely on its puzzle, which requires the puzzle's solution and payoff to satisfy the reader. On the second go-around, understanding what the blue text's significance is and determining the truth of the anomaly, I find that the actual plot, Michaels' character, and the monster itself are all thoroughly underwhelming.

The communication method is clever, but that's it. The article needs a much more satisfying ending to be more than just a clever gimmick hiding an underwhelming twist. While I'm not a puzzle fan, my first thought is to explore ways for the DOMC and Michaels to brainstorm and execute a means of properly containing or curing the anomaly rather than just establishing that it exists in an obfuscated fashion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: <3 <3 <3 Thank you so much! I'm really glad you enjoyed AND that the declass was helpful!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't overstate how much I love this article. I always enjoy format-screw SCPs, so this was right up my alley. The blue text worked especially well for me because it isn't quite a straightforward "bluetext is false"- having several statements that are not true in different ways during the inoculation was a stroke of genius. I had to really work at some parts to figure out what was happening in the article, but never to an annoying extent. Eventually I got most of it, and the declassification video was great as it spelt out some details I missed and clarified details I wasn't sure about.

This article gives me a very strong feeling of dread and helplessness, and while the SCP itself creeps me the hell out, I mostly feel terribly sad for Michaels. This is a great concept and you executed it fantastically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is really clever. Both in the way you authors and the in-universe Foundation handle it.

Easy +1.

Also, I like that logo and motto of the DoMc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Already found this to be a really creative and enjoyable article that perfectly showcased a promising and intriguing new Foundation department before watching the declassification, but after watching it and coming to understand the full implications of the interview, my admiration for this article has tripled. Wonderfully creepy concept executed fantastically well; easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this to be an incredibly enjoyable read. The blue text was a nice twist. Keep up the good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this one! I'm kind of totally a lazy bum, so I had to watch the declassification (which was good by the way.) to get the whole story. But even before that, I was ready to give a positive vote anyways, because the most recently annoying thing to me has been articles that revel in their confusingness so much that they're entirely unenjoyable unless you put in a significant amount of time to figure out every detail, and this article is both thoroughly enjoyable to simply read as is, and to read knowing the answer to the riddle!

+1

item.title: New DoMC Logo – Attribution
item.content:encoded:
Image Attribution

The Department of Miscommunications Logo was initially created by hawkguyy and reworked by JackalRelated, and has been released under a CC BY-SA 3.0 license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooooh, the blue text gimmick is clever, and I like how the article never reveals too much, making you figure it out.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The brother bit isn't a sob story, though.

+ show block
– hide block

It's all made up crap as part of the scripted conversation to indirectly say what the anomaly is: a literal parasite on the back of his head.

Aside from being in blue (aka purposely false) the description says that he is an only child.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little conflicted here. The blue-text thing is actually really clever re: format screw; so clever that I'm a little irritated that I didn't think of it myself (it's a natural progression from SCP-4000 and SCPs that signal they're lying to you). Using it to indicate which lines are literally false is a great trick, and you deploy it effectively throughout the article (writing "Safe" in blue was probably my favorite). I'm particularly enamored with how you established the 'rules' for the audience (the use of the inoculation-check with the false statements in blue was an inspired touch). The interview's dialogue is well-written, and I like how you imply a 'hidden story' using the blue text. Ultimately, I upvoted on that basis.

But… I feel like this could have been much stronger, particularly given all the vast opportunities this trick affords you. I never felt any sense of dread, nor did I ever find myself really compelled to figure out what the 'hidden story' was all about. Maybe it's more me just being jaded on contagious cognitohazards or stories about personnel in danger? But at the end, I was more intrigued by the method of story-telling than the story itself.

Don't take this as a strong criticism; I think the article is solid, competent, and effective. Just — for me — this was more of a polished 'proof of concept' than anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe what they mean is that the article was intentionally written to be confusing and non-linear to bait questions on what it means, so that the writer could happily gorge out the details that should have been more clear or hinted at in the story. Perhaps this person believes that the article was written to boost the authors ego under the comments that read "This is so clever!!" or "What does it meeeaaaannn??"

That's my interpretation, at least, not my opinion on the article itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Michaels is not in danger.

I love it when a single line can snap me into just the right headspace for an article. Fantastic job.

item.title: Dr. Michaels is not at a +100 rating.
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Michaels is not excited.

Wow, I don't think I've ever had an article hit 100 in such a short time. That's 100 votes in 10 days. Thanks, all!

And thank you to my co-author, Henzoid, for coming up with the idea and pushing it as much as you possibly could, keeping the project motivated and imaginative. Couldn't have done it without you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very cool, I give one custom reddit community award valued at 1000 reddit sheckles. The award is called “very sweg” and is a rainbow shibe inu commonly known as “doge”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what if, there is an anti-memetic agent that consumes all information about himself, and somehow Dr. Michaels is not affected by it

This is an interesting theory, but it misses the mark for a couple of reasons:

  1. The Department of Miscommunications doesn't specialize in antimemetics.
  2. If they use Veritants and Amnestics here then why not Mnestics too?

Also note, in the page header it says "we have designed this document" — meaning, the DoMc is in control of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what if, there is an anti-memetic agent that consumes all information about himself, and somehow Dr. Michaels is not affected by it, the easiest way to talk about it would be to say the opposite of what it is, or rather what it isn't, like to say that "it is round".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hell yeah, I can finally comment about this.

This was an amazing read, and the pieces in the puzzle were easy enough to figure out with a little bit of thought. Loved drawing for this, loved reading it, +1, +1, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took a couple rereads, but this is fridge horror done excellently. Masterfully crafted format screw, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: posting in a discussion thread for the first time to say I think this is great. took a little bit to process the formatting, but especially on a full reread after the intent clicks, man, the interview is tense as all get out and kind of masterfully crafted. good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not critique, just speculation.

+ show block
– hide block

There are a few things omitted as opposed to lied about in blue. The interviewer is unnamed and so is the doctor being quoted. I can't guess who the doctor might be in relation to Dr. Michaels.

The anomaly is concerned with Dr. Michaels' safety. Perhaps it is protective of Dr. Michaels. The anomaly may react adversely if Dr. Michaels expresses any form of distress. Notably, Safe as a containment class is not about how physically safe Dr. Michaels is. It's more about how safe it is to be around him, to be part of containing him.
Dr. Michaels is non-anomalous, and his only family members are his parents. The anomaly status of his parents is unstated.
His latest bout of fogginess began after the death of the D-class, but he has had similar feelings in the past, whenever he is away from his parents. He does not consider the death of the D-class traumatic for some reason.
The interviewer and Dr. Michaels are talking about someone other than Dr. Michaels' brother. Yet the interviewer does ask about a brother. The interviewer may have mentioned his family's son, which could only be him.
Dr. Michael describes his "brother" as a dead weight. That's a harsh thing to say about one's brother. I feel as if an attempt to parse this would be too sappy, as that's what ARD arrives at. The self-pity explanation I made up for this is too unsympathetic to really work with when coming with something that makes the most of this format.
Guessing from the pronouns, his father is hostile and he called (his mother? the anomaly?) a parasite. The interviewer wasn't the first (the first what?).
The interviewer never got a report back about the source of the sewage backup, because the task force was occupied with something else.

I don't know what to conclude from any of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
not very interesting at all if everything's safe and fine
– hide block

See, when the text is narrow and highlighted blue, that means it's a lie. I enjoyed this. My only genuine criticism is that there gets to be a bit too much description of the brother when the blue highlighting rather clearly indicates none of that is real. unless there's some hidden connection in the story of his brother that I completely missed, it is nice as character build, but I was too distracted by the fact that it was all smeared in blue to actually read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuuuuuuuck, this is like the epitome of fridge horror. Love it love it love it, there's so much to read into and decipher. Enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with everything the guy above me said, but also:

Correct me if I'm wrong but

+ show block
– hide block

It's a big purple (He says his favorite color is "pur-" so I'm just assuming) slime monster, or some gross thing. (It came out of a pipe, and it has to be cleaned up after, and the page is tagged as "amorphous" so, yeah.)

I'm pretty sure that's right

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, this is an interesting one

+ Possible spoilers and rampant speculation
- Try figuring it out for yourself first though, it's very fun

So the DoMc note establishes that the documentation is being written by them in a very specific way, that cannot be stated outright. The following note establishes what this means, i.e. some parts of the documentation are false and that these parts are highlighted with blue background. This is further suggested by the rest of the description, which also establishes that Michaels is clearly in danger.

Moving to the interview, it seems to be implied that SCP-4428, which presumably is the reason why the documentation must be like this, is a physical entity of some kind that follows Michaels (the background of the picture is redacted, the interviewer glances above Michaels for seemingly no reason). In order to follow the rules laid out by the documentation, Michaels and the interviewer talked the interview the way it is written, with the blue background added in post by DoMc to signify which parts were false. This is also implied by a couple of things, like how they tense up when they get close to describing 4428, how Michaels urges the interviewer to skip the color question (suggesting this is pre-planned in some way), and how the interviewer gestures at Michaels (notably in a fashion that avoids being recorded).
Going through the interview, it seems that 4428 causes a very real sensation of fogginess to Michaels. It appears that 4428 also killed the rest of Michaels’ team, hinting that it can be lethal. The conversation about Michaels’ brother seems to be loosely metaphorical to 4428, which acts like a parasite, as implied at the end of that particular conversation (with “him” probably meaning “it”, i.e. 4428). After this it is also implied that 4428 has affected other people. The end bit regarding the MTF also possibly hints at how 4228 was discovered.
The final note then implies that, even with the clever documentation of DoMc, the situation is still very challenging for Michaels (as is implied in the interview by how 4428 is seemingly called a “stressor”). Based on this, and some other stuff in the article, it is obvious that 4428 poses a great danger to him, most likely because it will kill him if its effects are triggered.

TL;DR, 4428 is a physical entity that acts like a parasite and is currently attached to Dr. Michaels, after having caused the deaths of his research team. It appears to kill its host when it is described in any way (including pictures or information about its properties). The Department of Miscommunications have figured this all out and use intentionally false, but highlighted, information in the documentation to imply what is going on, without triggering 4428’s effect. And Michaels is quite stressed about the whole deal.

Regardless of how right any of that is, this is an absolutely intriguing article. Amazing work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While there are inconsistencies with this explanation, I thoroughly enjoy it. It's one of my favorite theories so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's intentionally several explanations. While not the one used while writing (which the authors have requested not to spoil), this is my preferred one:

One explanation
– hide block

Jeremy works at Site-96, which in SCP-4098 holds the previous contents of Site-94. And what was SCP-4098 supposed to protect against, again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also don’t get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't completely understand this yet, but it's super well written and it definitely feels like there's something more to it even if it hasn't quite clicked with me yet. Big ol +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man this is a lot of fun. Not really much else to say beyond that. This got the gears turning in the ol' noggin and I enjoyed the shit out of it. Excellent stuff my dudes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's a theory that helped me figure out what was going on, but itself wasn't actually right:

Theory
– hide block

It's pretty likely the interview didn't actually happen.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: claim100, do not reply to your own comment. Instead, click on the options button in your previous comment, then the option to edit your post. Then you can edit the new information into your old post, to avoid appearing spammy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might have just clicked something. An anomalous parasite has affected Dr. Michaels making him believe he even had a brother.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I really want to understand it too. No vote until there's an explanation, then i'll make a decision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's okay, thanks for giving it a read!

I feel like it's one of those "ASK ME WHAT IT MEANS!!1" things

I'm not so sure what this statement is trying to get at, can you explain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to enjoy this… I really do. But I feel like it's one of those "ASK ME WHAT IT MEANS!!1" things, so I'm going to no vote for now. I'll reread it later and see if I can find away around that vibe

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a valid criticism, however, I will point out that Michaels' spiel is not necessarily there to act as a (for lack of a better term) sob story. There is a lot more going on under the surface than that, and you have to consider that maybe everything highlighted in blue should be taken with a huge grain of salt instead of simply being opposite. What are the characters really trying to communicate? Why are some specific words highlighted whereas others aren't?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much my exact thoughts. The entire article feels rushed, and it suffers a lot for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The blue opposite text is one of the better format screws I’ve seen recently, but I didn’t find Jeremy Michaels to be a particularly interesting character. The way the interview is structured and Michaels talks feels designed to force a sympathetic response, either by rapidly building Michaels as a character or by getting the reader to relate to the article’s themes of emotional neediness and family ties. But it happens too fast and thus causes me to notice the artificiality of that character and the hasty injection of those themes.

item.title: Re: Dr. Michaels is not in danger.
item.content:encoded:
What better way to celebrate SpookyMonth than with an article about A COMPLETELY NORMAL RESEARCHER JUST LIVING HIS LIFE

What a fun ride, collaborating with Floppy was great, and the DoMC is always so exciting to write with.

Spoilers?
Nah, not really

There is an intended story, but because of the nature of the piece, there are many ways to interpret what's going on. To those who can piece together all the subtle clues, I salute you, but it's not easy, or necessary to enjoy the piece.

Actual Spoilers?
Well... almost

I will say this much. One of these comments is almost exactly correct, down to the detail.

Ok seriously this time, spoilers
Yep, I'm serious this time

I did a declass of the piece! Go watch it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that was an interesting read; the blue opposite text was a really fun gimmick +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, this is excellent and a really good addition to the current Department of Miscommunication material.

item.title: Dr. Michaels is not in danger.
item.content:encoded:
SCP-4428: Dr. Michaels is not in danger.
Authors: Henzoid and Lt Flops

This was a collab between Henzoid and I. It's the first time I've written for the DoMc and I can't put into words how thrilling it was.

There will be no spoilers. Hope you enjoy, and happy Spooktober 👻!
